coredata.dc:title: PTB7-Th sensitized by AA-CDs for photoelectrochemical protein assay
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Suying
Li, Mengjie
Shen, Guohao
Wang, Qihui
Tang, Jing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor for C-reactive protein (CRP) assay was successfully constructed by using poly{4,8-bis[5-(2-ethylhexyl)thiophen-2-yl]benzo[1,2-b,4,5-b′]dithiophene-2,6-diyl-alt-3-fluoro-2-[(2-ethylhexyl)carbonyl] thieno[3,4–b]-thiophene-4,6-diyl} (PTB7-Th) as the photoactive substrate and carbon dots modified with amino acid (AA-CDs) as the signal sensitizer. Impressively, the designed AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure presented a strong and stable photocurrent response without any external electron donor, which was mainly derived from the inherent self-supplied electron characteristic of PTB7-Th and the high sensitizing effect of AA-CDs towards PTB7-Th. The obtained PEC signal produced from the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure was even larger than the sum of PTB7-Th and AA-CDs. Besides, the special recognition between CRP and its aptamer endowed the PEC aptasensor with excellent specificity, suggesting that the PEC aptasensing platform embraced strong anti-interference ability. Accordingly, the PEC aptasensor fabricated here confirmed a detection limit (LOD) of 0.33 pg/mL for CRP assay within the detection range of 1 pg/mL to 1 µg/mL. Importantly, this system offered an ideal PEC aptamer sensing platform based on the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure for CRP determination, laying the foundation for its application in the detection of real protein samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-reactive protein
Aptamer
PTB7-Th
Carbon dots
Photoelectrochemical aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of PTB7-Th (A) and Au NPs (C), EDX spectrum of PTB7-Th (B), TEM image of AA-CDs (D) and UV–vis spectra of PTB7-Th (E) and AA-CDs (F).
Effects of (A) the wavelength range of irradiation light source and (B) the deposition time of Au NPs on photocurrent signal. Error bars represent standard deviations of three experiments (N = 3).
PEC signals of (a) AA-CDs, (b) PTB7-Th, (c) the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure.

PEC signals of (a) bare GCE, (b) PTB7-Th/GCE, (c) AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (d) Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (e) S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (f) HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (g) CRP/HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE.

EIS (A) and CV (B) responses of (a) bare GCE, (b) PTB7-Th/GCE, (c) AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (d) Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (e) S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (f) HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (g) CRP/HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE.
EIS responses (A), conductivity values (B) and the calculated values of Cdl (C) of (a) the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (b) the S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (c) the HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE.

(A) PEC responses with various CRP concentrations. (B) Linear relationship between the PEC value and the logarithm of CRP concentration. Error bars in Fig. 6B represent standard deviations of three experiments (N = 3).
(A) Stability investigation of PEC aptasensor. (B) Selectivity of PEC aptasensor towards different targets. Error bars in Fig. 7B represent standard deviations of three experiments (N = 3).
Schematic representations of PEC aptasensor (A) and electron-transfer mechanism (B).
Comparison of different detection methods for CRP.

head.ce:title: PTB7-Th sensitized by AA-CDs for photoelectrochemical protein assay

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor for C-reactive protein (CRP) assay was successfully constructed by using poly{4,8-bis[5-(2-ethylhexyl)thiophen-2-yl]benzo[1,2-b,4,5-b′]dithiophene-2,6-diyl-alt-3-fluoro-2-[(2-ethylhexyl)carbonyl] thieno[3,4–b]-thiophene-4,6-diyl} (PTB7-Th) as the photoactive substrate and carbon dots modified with amino acid (AA-CDs) as the signal sensitizer. Impressively, the designed AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure presented a strong and stable photocurrent response without any external electron donor, which was mainly derived from the inherent self-supplied electron characteristic of PTB7-Th and the high sensitizing effect of AA-CDs towards PTB7-Th. The obtained PEC signal produced from the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure was even larger than the sum of PTB7-Th and AA-CDs. Besides, the special recognition between CRP and its aptamer endowed the PEC aptasensor with excellent specificity, suggesting that the PEC aptasensing platform embraced strong anti-interference ability. Accordingly, the PEC aptasensor fabricated here confirmed a detection limit (LOD) of 0.33 pg/mL for CRP assay within the detection range of 1 pg/mL to 1 µg/mL. Importantly, this system offered an ideal PEC aptamer sensing platform based on the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure for CRP determination, laying the foundation for its application in the detection of real protein samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: C-reactive protein (CRP) is an acute phase protein synthesized by the liver when the body is infected and the tissue damaged [1]. It is a critical inflammatory marker that can activate the complement system and promote the phagocytosis of phagocytic cells, thus playing a protective role during the natural immune process of body [2]. CRP is usually utilized to assess the degree of inflammation and monitor the activity of inflammatory diseases [3]. The detection of CRP possesses important reference value for clinical diagnosis and treatment evaluation. Therefore, it is extremely momentous to develop a kind of analysis method with high sensitivity, strong anti-interference ability and stability for CRP assay. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor is an emerging electrochemical analysis technology that combines the high sensitivity of luminescence analysis, the high controllability of electrochemistry and the high specificity of biosensing technology [4–6]. PEC biosensor has shown broad development prospects in fields such as life analysis, food safety and environmental monitoring due to the advantages of simple construction, excellent stability, high sensitivity and strong specificity [7–12]. It’s exceedingly expected to design a PEC sensor for detection of CRP with exceptional analytical performance.

Usually, it is necessary to supplement electron donor/electron acceptor by the methods of additional addition and generation in situ for ensuring the normal operation of PEC sensors [13,14]. Although the above measures significantly upgrade the photocurrent intensity, there are still certain serious limitations, such as the limited number and uneven distribution of electron donor/electron acceptor. More importantly, the electron transfer between photoactive materials and electron donor/electron acceptor is an intermolecular transmission process that tremendously reduces the efficiency and stability of electron transfer. Poly{4,8-bis[5-(2-ethylhexyl) thiophen-2-yl]benzo[1,2-b,4,5-b′]dithiophene-2,6-diyl-alt-3-fluoro-2-[(2-ethylhexyl)carbonyl] thieno[3,4–b]-thiophene-4,6-diyl} (PTB7-Th) is a typical donor–acceptor type photoactive material, which embraces both the electron-rich donor unit and the electron-deficient acceptor unit inside its molecule [15]. The molecular structural characteristics endow PTB7-Th with the ability of intramolecular electron transport, thus reducing transmission distance and energy loss as well as enhancing photoelectric conversion efficiency and photocurrent intensity stability. Resultly, PTB7-Th can generate stable photocurrent response without external addition of electron donor/electron acceptor. However, the insufficient PEC intensity of PTB7-Th partly limits its application in PEC biosensors. It is urgent to modify PTB7-Th for maintaining the PEC signal stability and further improving the PEC intensity.

The sensitization strategy is a classic and effective method for improving the PEC intensity of photoactive materials. In recent years, PTB7-Th has been sensitized by some sensitizers such as fullerene, polyaniline, CdTe quantum dots and [Ru(dcbpy)2dppz]2+ for obtaining high photocurrent intensity in PEC biosensor field [16–19]. Nevertheless, the existing sensitization strategies based on PTB7-Th existed some shortcomings, such as toxic raw materials, complex synthesis pathway and cumbersome loading method. It’s expected to find a kind of novel sensitizer towards PTB7-Th that could avoid the above-mentioned weaknesses and increase the related PEC intensity. Herein, low toxic carbon dots (CDs) modified with amino acid (AA-CDs) is a type of carbon nanomaterials obtained by surface modification of CDs with amino acid molecules. Compared with ordinary CDs, AA-CDs synthesized by hydrothermal method possesses outstanding characteristics including better water solubility, stronger biocompatibility, enhanced functionality and tunable optical properties [20–22]. Simultaneously, its film-forming property is also excellent, which can be directly coated on the sensing surface without the need of complex loading processes. Therefore, AA-CDs was chosen as the sensitizer towards PTB7-Th to generate the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure by the simple method of layer-by-layer modification, thus obtaining strong and stable PEC response for construction of PEC sensing platform.

In the present study, a PEC aptasensor on the basis of the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure was constructed using PTB7-Th as the photoactive substrate and AA-CDs as the signal sensitizer, achieving the quantitative detection of CRP. PTB7-Th and AA-CDs were coated on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) through the layer-by-layer modification method, thus obtaining the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure and producing the strong and stable PEC response. Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) were then electrodeposited on the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE interface. The aptamer of CRP (S1) was then immobilized on the above electrode surface by the classical bonding interaction of Au-N, followed by blocking with 1-hexanethiol (HT). The introduction of target CRP significantly enhanced the spatial resistance owing to the generation of the complex structure of CRP-aptamer, and thus weakened the PEC response produced from sensitization strategy, which realized the CRP detection with high specificity and stability.

Poly{4,8-bis[5-(2-ethylhexyl)thiophen-2-yl]benzo[1,2-b:4,5-b’]dithiophene-2,6-diyl-alt-3-fluoro-2-[(2-ethylhexyl)-carbonyl] thieno[3,4–b]thiophene-4,6-diyl} (PTB7-Th) was obtained from Luminescence Technology Corp. (Taiwan, China). Glucose, tris-HCl buffer, 1-hexanethiol (HT) and potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) were acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroform (CHCl3) and C-reactive protein (CRP) were supplied by Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Chongqing, China) and Oukai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China), respectively. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) and hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) were provided by China National Medical Co. (Beijing, China). Potassium chloride (KCl), l-leucine and potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) were acquired from Kelong Chemical Inc. (Chengdu, China). Ultrapure water was prepared by M10UP analytical ultrapure water machine. 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.0) was composed of 0.1 M KCl, 0.1 M Na2HPO4 as well as 0.1 M KH2PO4. The oligonucleotide sequence was obtained from General Bio Inc. (Anhui, China) and listed as follows: 5′-CGA AGG GGA TTC GAG GGG TGA TTG CGT GCT CCA TTT GGT GTT TTT TTT TTT T-NH2-3′.

PEC measurements were carried out on a CHI 660E electrochemistry workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, China) attached to a TPEC10W LED light source. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were also performed on the same workstation. The three-electrode system involved a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode. The morphologies of nanomaterials were investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM, SEM5000, China) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra of nanomaterials were recorded with a DR6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (HACH Company, Colorado, USA).

Initially, 1 mg of PTB7-Th solid was added to 3 mL of chloroform solution. Then, the mixture was subjected to continuous ultrasonic treatment for 10 min to ensure that PTB7-Th was evenly dispersed in chloroform. Finally, the prepared PTB7-Th solution with blackish green was stored at 4 °C for future use.

AA-CDs were synthesized using glucose as a carbon source through the classical hydrothermal process. First, 0.03 g of glucose and 0.015 g of l-leucine were added to 50 mL of ultrapure water and then the mixture was sonicated for 15 mins. The solution was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10h. After heating, the solution was allowed to cool to room temperature and centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 30 mins (three times) followed by dialysis with ultrapure water for 12 h to obtain the final product AA-CDs. Finally, the obtained AA-CDs were stored at 4 °C in the dark for future use.

The construction procedure of the PEC aptasensor was shown in Scheme 1 A. First, the GCE was carefully polished using α-Al2O3 and thoroughly rinsed with ultrapure water. Next, 5 µL of PTB7-Th solution and 5 µL of AA-CDs solution were sequentially dropped onto the electrode interface, and thus the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE could be obtained. Following this, Au NPs were deposited onto the above electrode surface using 0.1 % HAuCl4 solution as the Au source. Subsequently, 10 µL of 2 µM S1 solution was added and incubated overnight at 4 °C, allowing S1 to be immobilized on the surface of Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE via the link function of Au-N bonding. Afterwards, 10 µL of 0.1 mM HT was applied to block nonspecific binding sites. Finally, different concentrations of target CRP were added and then incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The introduced CRP was specifically recognized with its aptamer S1, thereby forming the complex structure of CRP-aptamer that could dramatically quench the PEC signal for detection of CRP.
The PEC measurement was conducted in 5 mL of 0.1 M PBS. The LED lamp with a wavelength of 365–370 nm was used as the excitation light source and switched “off–on-off” under 0.0 V potential.

PTB7-Th, AA-CDs and Au NPs acted the vital roles during the construction of PEC aptasensor, which were characterized by SEM, TEM, EDX spectrum and UV–vis spectra. Fig. 1 A revealed the SEM image of PTB7-Th. The clearly block structure with several stacking layers could be found for PTB7-Th from Fig. 1A. Also, these elements including C, O, F as well as S were recorded in EDX spectrum, which was shown in Fig. 1B. The SEM image of Au NPs along with the average size of 300 nm was depicted in Fig. 1C, where Au NPs originated from electrodeposition. In addition, Fig. 1D presented the TEM image of AA-CDs, which possessed the uniform diameter of 3 nm. And the result was consistent with the literature [23]. Besides, the UV–vis absorption spectra of PTB7-Th and AA-CDs were shown in Fig. 1E and Fig. 1F, respectively. The characteristic peaks in Fig. 1E were clearly observed at 240, 313, 626, and 697 nm. The absorption band in Fig. 1F was found at 275 nm. All results verified the successful preparation of materials used in this work.

The wavelength range of irradiation light source and the deposition time of Au NPs possessed significant impact on the photocurrent intensity and the detection performance. Fig. 2 A presented the effect of the wavelength range of irradiation light source on the PEC response. Specifically, the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE was performed PEC measurement under different wavelength ranges. The maximal PEC signal about −0.54 µA could be obtained when the wavelength range of light source was selected as 365–370 nm. Thus, the LED lamp with the wavelength of 365–370 nm was selected. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, with increasing the deposition time of Au NPs from 0-2 s, the PEC intensity of the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE gradually enhanced. However, when the deposition time of Au NPs prolonged further, the related PEC signal tended to decrease. Therefore, 2 s was selected as the optimal deposition time.

Scheme 1B illustrated the theoretical electron transfer mechanism. The AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure generated on the surface of working electrode by continuous coating and drying of PTB7-Th and AA-CDs. Under illumination with the wavelength of 365–370 nm, these electrons within PTB7-Th jumped from the unit of electron donor to the unit of electron acceptor. Subsequently, electrons located in its acceptor unit migrated to the conduction band (CB) of AA-CDs. Meanwhile, these electrons within AA-CDs were also excited by illumination energy, which could immediately transferred from the valence band (VB) to its CB. And then these photogenerated electrons in the CB of AA-CDs would be captured by dissolved oxygen in electrolyte solution, thus forming superoxide radicals. Importantly, the electrolyte solution was the PBS solution without any electron donor. An excellent initial PEC intensity could be obtained by construction of the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure without addition of the external electron donors.

For the purpose of sensitive assay, a high PEC response produced from the photoactive material is necessary. Therefore, PEC signals of different materials were carefully detected for comparison. Concretely, three kinds of photoactive materials including AA-CDs, PTB7-Th and the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure were modified onto the working electrode surfaces. PEC measurements were performed under the same condition, where no electron donor was added into the electrolyte solution (PBS). The measurement results were clearly shown in Fig. 3 . Curve a presented the photocurrent response of the AA-CDs/GCE that was nearly 0.007 µA. Curve b illustrated that the PEC response of the PTB7-Th/GCE was about −0.38 µA. When the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure was coated onto the GCE surface, the related PEC signal enhanced significantly and the recorded data was −0.54 µA (curve c), which was even larger than the sum of curve a and curve b. The remarkable increasing in PEC signal was mainly attributed to the construction of the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure. Therefore, the proposed sensitized structure of AA-CDs/PTB7-Th was selected as the photoactive material for this PEC aptasensor.

The PEC aptasensor based on the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure was explored by recording the PEC signal of each modification step, which was presented in Fig. 4 . As shown in curve a, almost no photocurrent response was obtained for the bare GCE. A relatively strong photocurrent signal (−0.38 µA) was observed at PTB7-Th/GCE (curve b). Upon addition of AA-CDs, the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure generated on the GCE surface and thus the related PEC signal significantly increased (−0.54 µA), which was appeared at curve c. The reason for this might be attributed to the fact that the sensitized structure could promote electron transmission and suppress charge recombination. When electrodeposition of Au NPs was performed, the further enhanced PEC signal was achieved (curve d). After introduction of S1 (the aptamer of CRP) and HT, the PEC responses gradually reduced, which appeared at curve e and curve f. Eventually, the added target CRP could specifically recognized with S1, thus generating the complex structure of CRP-aptamer that could dramatically quench the PEC signal (curve g). Considering the above results, we might make a conclusion that the PEC aptasensor based on the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure was successfully constructed.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were the effective methods to explore the interface property of different modified working electrodes. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the semicircular portion of bare GCE was small (curve a), implying that the electron transfer resistance (R et) was weak because the semicircular diameter of high frequency region is corresponding to R et. After PTB7-Th and AA-CDs successively coated on GCE, the related semicircular portion of EIS enhanced (curve b and curve c), which confirmed the value of R et successively grew. As presented in curve d, the R et value of the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE dramatically reduced because Au NPs from electrodeposition could promote electron transfer. When S1 and HT were modified on the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE surface in succession, their semicircular diameters continued to increase, which were shown in curve e and curve f. After modification with the target CRP, the R et value of curve g (CRP/HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE) also further raised owing to the increased spatial resistance.

As appeared in Fig. 5B, the bare GCE (curve a) showed a pair of reversible oxidation–reduction peaks. The peak current of PTB7-Th/GCE significantly reduced (curve b) relative to the former. After coated with AA-CDs, the peak current of curve c (AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE) also further decreased. The Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE presented an enhanced peak current (curve d) compared to curve c. This observation was attributed to the promoting ability towards electron transfer by Au NPs. The peak currents of the S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE (curve e) and the HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE (curve f) decreased in succession. Finally, when the target CRP was captured by its aptamer (S1), the recorded peak current (curve g) downgraded once more due to the electron transfer blockage. These results suggested that the PEC aptasensor was fabricated successfully.

Conductivity is a critical parameter that describes the ease of charge flow, which can be used to evaluate the conductivity property of modified electrodes. The related conductivity of the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, the S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE and the HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE were investigated by using EIS measurement and the formula of
σ =
L
R ct × A
, where L is the thickness of electrode material, A represents the effective area of electrode and R ct is the fitted charge transfer resistance. The related R ct values of the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, the S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE and the HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE were shown in Fig. 6 A. The L values of different materials could be obtained form the following references [24–26]. The value of A is calculated to be 12.56 mm2. Therefore, the conductivity values (Fig. 6B) of (a) the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (b) the S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, (c) the HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE are 56.2, 10.3 and 9.7 nS/cm, respectively. These results confirmed that the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE possessed the wonderful conductivity, and the adding of S1 and HT would give rise to the gradual reduction in conductivity.

The double layer capacitance (Cdl) is an important parameter to accurately reflect the electrochemical performance of electrode surface. The related Cdl of the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE, the S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE and the HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE were investigated by using the classical CV method along with the scanning rate from 10 mV/s to 70 mV/s in the non-Faraday current range, and the detection buffer solution was 0.1 M HAC-NaAC. As shown in Fig. 6C, the peak current densities of three electrode were linearly related to their scanning rate, and the Cdl value of the Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE (curve a) was dramatically higher than those of the S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE (curve b) and the HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE (curve c). The result indicated that the modification of S1 and HT would lead to the continuous decrease in the electrochemical performance of the modified electrode.

The PEC aptasensor was fabricated based on the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure under the optimal experimental condition for CRP detection. The photocurrent responses gradually downgraded with the increasing of CRP concentration from 1 pg/mL to 1 µg/mL, and the related result was presented in Fig. 7 A. And Fig. 7B presented the relationship between the PEC values (I) and the logarithm of CRP concentrations (lgc). The linear curve was obtained from linear fitting and the corresponding linear equation was I = 0.058lgc-0.345 (r = 0.998) relevant to the range of 1 pg/mL-1 µg/mL as well as the detection limit (LOD) of 0.33 pg/mL. LOD was calculated based on LOD = 3SB /m, where SB (0.4 %) is the standard deviation (SD) of there blank measures (N = 3), and m (0.036) is the analytical sensitivity, which can be represented as the slope of calibration plot at lower concentration ranges. Compared with some published data (Table 1 ), the proposed PEC aptasensor embraced a wide linear detection range and a low LOD, implying this aptasensor possessed an excellent application prospect.

Stability and selectivity were extremely essential parameters for evaluating the performance of the sensor. To investigate the stability of the as-fabricated aptasensor, CRP with 1 ng/mL was modified onto the sensing interface and the related PEC measurements were performed. As observed in Fig. 8 A, the time-dependent photocurrent intensities of the PEC aptasensor were recorded under the optimal condition. It was clear that no obvious change in PEC responses of the CRP/HT/S1/Au NPs/AA-CDs/PTB7-Th/GCE was observed during the 10-circles. Furthermore, the standard deviation (SD) of 0.24 % and the RSD of 0.75 % could be obtained after calculation (N = 10), which implied a good stability of this PEC aptasensor. In addition, to investigate the selectivity of this PEC aptasensor towards CRP, some possible interferences including thrombin (TB), bovine serum albumin (BSA), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), prostate-specific antigen (PSA), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), tumour regulatory (TR), lead ion (Pb2+) and mercury ion (Hg2+) were chosen as the interfering substances for performing selectivity exploration. Here, the concentration of CRP was 10 ng/mL and the concentrations of these interfering substances were 1 µg/mL. After incubation of interferences, these PEC signals recorded in Fig. 8B were almost no difference compared to that of blank. As was evident, the obviously downgraded photocurrent intensity could be found when 10 ng/mL CRP was captured. The excellent selectivity of this PEC aptasensor was mainly attributed to the good specificity between CRP and its aptamer.

In order to evaluate the prospective application of the proposed PEC aptasensor towards CRP detection, the conventional standard addition method was adopted in this work. Real serum samples were used for this experiment. Specifically, CRP with various concentrations were added into the three-diluted real serum samples and then these samples were detected by the aptasensor proposed here. The found CRP concentration could be calculated by combining the detected PEC signal with the linear equation. The recovery of CRP was listed in Table 2 , falling in the range of 96 %–104 %, showing that the PEC aptasensor possessed a good potential for application.

In summary, a simple PEC aptasensor based on the AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure was put forward for the determination of CRP. Firstly, PTB7-Th as a typical donor–acceptor type photoactive material was selected as the photoactive substrate, which could produce stable photocurrent response without external addition of electron donor/acceptor. Secondly, AA-CDs synthesized by hydrothermal method with glucose as carbon source was chosen as the effective sensitizer towards PTB7-Th. The construction of AA-CDs/PTB7-Th sensitized structure via the layer-by-layer modification method has greatly boosted the PEC performance, thus generating strong and stable photocurrent response for PEC sensor. Thirdly, the specific recognition between CRP and its aptamer tremendously upgrade the selectivity of this PEC aptasensor. Accordingly, the PEC aptasensor proposed here was finally constructed toward selective and stable monitoring of a critical inflammatory marker CRP, which presented a wide detection range along with a low LOD. This work provided a novel and environmental PTB7-Th-based sensitized structure and also offered a profound insight for detection of CRP.

Suying Yang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Mengjie Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Guohao Shen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Qihui Wang: Software, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis. Jing Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2024M753863).
